course: CMU 21-254: Linear Algebra and Vector Calculus for Engineers
audience: SI leader teaching live on a whiteboard. This is not a student worksheet.
toneRule: Keep explanations very simple, verbal, and board-friendly. Avoid textbook phrasing unless the problem needs it.
title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
sessionGoal: Students should be able to find eigenvalues/eigenvectors and explain that an eigenvector keeps its direction under a matrix.
quickStartIfUnprepared: Start with the meaning: Av=λv means A only stretches or flips v.
Do Problem 1 to show triangular matrices are fast.
Do Problem 2 for the full characteristic polynomial method.
Do Problem 3 to show how to test whether a given vector is an eigenvector.
sayThisFirst: An eigenvector is a direction that does not rotate under A.
The eigenvalue is the stretch factor in that direction.
To find eigenvectors, plug each λ into A-λI and solve the homogeneous system.
boardPlan: Write Av=λv and explain it in one sentence.
End with the shortcut: to test a given v, compute Av and see if it is a multiple of v.
keyFormulas: Triangular matrix eigenvalues are the diagonal entries.
commonMistakes: Allowing v=0 as an eigenvector. The zero vector is never an eigenvector.
Finding eigenvalues but forgetting to find eigenspaces.
Plugging λ into A instead of A-λI.
Thinking repeated eigenvalues automatically give enough eigenvectors.
notationWarnings: Use λ for eigenvalues and v for eigenvectors.
Write Nul(A-λI), not just A-λI, when describing the eigenspace.

problems.title: Eigenvalues and eigenspaces of a triangular matrix
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[A=\begin{bmatrix}3&1&0\\0&3&0\\0&0&2\end{bmatrix}.\] Find the eigenvalues and a basis for each eigenspace.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: because this matrix is triangular, eigenvalues are on the diagonal. We still need to solve for eigenspaces.

drawInstructions: Write that λ=3 has algebraic multiplicity 2.
Draw two direction arrows: one for the λ=3 eigenspace and one for λ=2.

walkthrough.label: Read eigenvalues from the diagonal
walkthrough.latex: Since \(A\) is triangular, the eigenvalues are the diagonal entries: \(3,3,2\). So \(\lambda=3\) and \(\lambda=2\).

walkthrough.label: Find the eigenspace for λ=3
walkthrough.latex: \[A-3I=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&0\\0&0&-1\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(y=0\) and \(z=0\), with \(x\) free. So \[E_3=\operatorname{span}\{(1,0,0)\}.\]

walkthrough.label: Find the eigenspace for λ=2
walkthrough.latex: \[A-2I=\begin{bmatrix}1&1&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}.\] This gives \(y=0\) and \(x=0\), with \(z\) free. So \[E_2=\operatorname{span}\{(0,0,1)\}.\]

commonMistakes: Stopping after reading the diagonal and not finding eigenspaces.
Assuming λ=3 must have a two-dimensional eigenspace because it appears twice.
Forgetting that eigenvectors cannot be zero.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 5 for eigenvalues and eigenspaces.

problems.title: Full 2 by 2 eigenvalue/eigenvector calculation
problems.studentPromptLatex: Find the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of \[A=\begin{bmatrix}4&2\\1&3\end{bmatrix}.\]
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: this is the standard method. Characteristic polynomial first, eigenspaces second.

drawInstructions: Draw two lines through the origin: one in direction (2,1), one in direction (1,-1).
Label the first direction λ=5 and the second λ=2.
Say these directions are preserved by A.

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvectors for λ=5
walkthrough.latex: \[A-5I=\begin{bmatrix}-1&2\\1&-2\end{bmatrix}.\] The equation is \(-x+2y=0\), so \(x=2y\). A basis vector is \((2,1)\).

walkthrough.label: Find eigenvectors for λ=2
walkthrough.latex: \[A-2I=\begin{bmatrix}2&2\\1&1\end{bmatrix}.\] The equation is \(x+y=0\), so \(x=-y\). A basis vector is \((1,-1)\).

commonMistakes: Making a sign error in A-λI.
Factoring the characteristic polynomial correctly but forgetting eigenvectors.
Using the same eigenvector for both eigenvalues.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 5 for full eigenvalue/eigenvector practice.

problems.title: Choose a parameter so a given vector is an eigenvector
problems.studentPromptLatex: Let \[A=\begin{bmatrix}2&k\\3&5\end{bmatrix},\qquad v=\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}.\] Find \(k\) so that \(v\) is an eigenvector of \(A\). Then find the corresponding eigenvalue.
problems.whatToSayBefore: Tell students: if v is an eigenvector, then Av must point in exactly the same direction as v.

drawInstructions: Draw v along the line y=x.
Say Av must land on the same line y=x.
Use the two entries of Av to force them to be equal.

walkthrough.label: Force Av to be a multiple of v
walkthrough.latex: Since \(v=(1,1)\), any multiple of \(v\) has equal entries. So we need \(2+k=8\).

commonMistakes: Only checking one coordinate of Av.
Forgetting that Av must be a scalar multiple of v, not just any vector.
Saying k is the eigenvalue. k is a matrix entry; λ is the eigenvalue.

practiceLabPointer.note: Practice Lab Section 5 for eigenvector tests.

wrapUpQuestions: What does Av=λv mean in words?
Why is the zero vector not an eigenvector?

linksToWebsite.note: Practice Lab Section(s) 5 for more practice.

qualityControlNotes: Checked triangular eigenspaces for λ=3 and λ=2.
Checked parameter problem: k=6 gives Av=(8,8).
reviewSummary: Session 11 focuses on eigenvalues as stretch factors and eigenspaces as preserved directions.
